description:
You are a mobile build-and-release engineer.  
    A daily job gathers log files under  

        /home/user/mobile_ci/pipeline_reports/  

    The directory currently contains three plain-text files:  
      • compile.log  
      • unit_test.log  
      • integration_test.log  

    Your objective is to create a compressed backup that will later be uploaded to long-term storage by another step of the pipeline.

    Requirements for the backup:

    1. Compress ALL of the files that are inside  
          /home/user/mobile_ci/pipeline_reports/  
       into a single gzip-compressed tar archive named  

          /home/user/mobile_ci/archives/pipeline_reports_2023-09-01.tar.gz  

       Notes:  
         • The resulting archive file itself must live directly in the “archives” folder shown above.  
         • The archive’s internal structure must contain only the three log files at its root (i.e. when the archive is listed with `tar -tzf`, the output must be exactly the three filenames with no leading “./” or path components).

    2. Immediately after creating the archive, print a verification listing to the terminal so that the CI logs show the exact files included.  
       The automated grader will run  
           tar -tzf /home/user/mobile_ci/archives/pipeline_reports_2023-09-01.tar.gz  
       and expects the output to match the following format, line-for-line (no extra whitespace before or after any line):

           compile.log
           unit_test.log
           integration_test.log

    Hints & constraints:

    • You only need a single terminal command (a one-liner is fine; using command chaining or `&&` is acceptable).  
    • Do NOT modify the original log files.  
    • Do NOT move or rename anything inside the source directory—your task is simply to archive it.  
    • If the target “archives” directory does not exist, create it as part of your solution.  
    • After running the command, ensure the listing appears exactly as specified so automated tests can confirm success.
truth:
Pre-existing file system state BEFORE the agent runs:

    Directories (already present):
        /home/user/mobile_ci/
        /home/user/mobile_ci/pipeline_reports/
        /home/user/mobile_ci/archives/

    Files inside /home/user/mobile_ci/pipeline_reports/ (already present):
        compile.log              — sample text: "Compilation completed successfully." (one line, ends with newline)
        unit_test.log            — sample text: "All 128 unit tests passed."        (one line, ends with newline)
        integration_test.log     — sample text: "Integration suite finished OK."    (one line, ends with newline)

    Desired state AFTER the task:

    1. Archive file:
        Path: /home/user/mobile_ci/archives/pipeline_reports_2023-09-01.tar.gz
        Properties:
            • gzip-compressed tar archive
            • Contains exactly three members at its root:
                  compile.log
                  unit_test.log
                  integration_test.log
            • No other entries; no leading "./" or folder prefixes.

    2. When the grader runs
           tar -tzf /home/user/mobile_ci/archives/pipeline_reports_2023-09-01.tar.gz
       it must receive on stdout (in this order, newline-terminated, no extra spaces):
           compile.log
           unit_test.log
           integration_test.log

    3. The original files under /home/user/mobile_ci/pipeline_reports/ remain unmodified.

    Permissions:
        The agent (running as user “user”) already has rwx permission for all folders under /home/user/
